serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Shabnam Masala is approximately 250 g, typically served alongside rice or Indian bread such as naan or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mushroom curry / vegetable masala composite
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Shabnam Masala is a traditional North Indian dish of mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus) and green peas in a spiced tomato-cream gravy. No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for this exact dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN entries for mushroom curry and mixed vegetable masala, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for button mushrooms (FDC ID 169251) and green peas (FDC ID 170419), and weighted to reflect a typical recipe composition of approximately 40% mushrooms, 15% peas, 20% tomato-onion gravy base, 10% cream/yogurt, and 15% oil and spices. Energy and macros are consistent with published values for comparable North Indian vegetable curries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed vegetable curry / mushroom-based curry analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Shabnam Masala. GI estimated at 40 based on analogous mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values of 35–45 for low-fat vegetable curries with legumes. The presence of green peas (GI ~48), mushrooms (very low GI), and a cream-tomato gravy base supports a composite GI in the low range. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (40 × 7.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 7.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~775 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a restaurant portion of Shabnam Masala can contribute approximately 775 mg sodium, representing roughly one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-salt preparation or limiting portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (~7.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Shabnam Masala has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 40 and a moderate glycemic load of 7.5 per standard serving, making it a suitable curry option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with low-GI accompaniments such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti and portion sizes are controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream or yogurt (~2.4 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat contributed by cream or yogurt in Shabnam Masala is moderate at approximately 2.4 g per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with low-fat yogurt instead of cream and monitor overall saturated fat intake across the meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shabnam Masala is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry whose name translates loosely as 'dew-drop spice blend', evoking the delicate, moist texture of its primary ingredient — button mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus). The dish typically combines sliced mushrooms and green peas in a rich, aromatic gravy made from sautéed onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala, finished with cream or yogurt to produce a velvety, mildly spiced sauce. The flavour profile is earthy, slightly tangy from the tomatoes, and warmly spiced without being aggressively hot. Shabnam Masala is served as a main-course side dish alongside steamed basmati rice, naan, roti, or paratha, and is a popular choice in North Indian restaurants across India and the Indian diaspora in Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~98 kcal per 100 g), useful amounts of plant protein (~3.8 g), dietary fibre (~2.1 g), and micronutrients including iron, B vitamins, and vitamin C from the tomato-vegetable base. Its low glycemic index (~40) makes it appropriate for glycaemic management. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: Shabnam Masala is a recognised North Indian vegetarian curry but no single ICMR-NIN or USDA entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mushroom curry and mixed vegetable masala entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Shabnam Masala is a North Indian mushroom-and-pea curry. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central ingredient data as no single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish; source_match_type is 'adapted'. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~40) based on comparable mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as this is a home-style vegetable curry with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN vegetable curry data. The dish is served at Dugout - Circular Road on field survey data at SGD 15.50. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided in accordance with Singaporean cuisine multilingual rules, as this dish is served in Singapore. Chinese transliteration (沙布南马萨拉 / 沙布南馬薩拉) is a phonetic rendering; no established Chinese culinary name exists for this specific dish.
